summary:
The Cortaillod culture was a Middle and Late Neolithic culture of the western Swiss plateau and adjacent regions, dating to approximately 4500–3500 BCE. It represents the primary Neolithic cultural tradition of the area that would become Switzerland and is best known for its lake-dwelling settlements — pile-dwellings built on platforms over the shallow margins of Alpine and pre-Alpine lakes. These waterlogged sites have preserved organic materials with exceptional fidelity, providing an unusually detailed record of Neolithic daily life.

Cortaillod communities practiced mixed farming, cultivating wheat, barley, lentils, and flax while herding cattle, pigs, sheep, and goats. The lake margins where they lived offered access to fish, waterfowl, and other lacustrine resources that supplemented the agricultural economy. The waterlogged conditions at Cortaillod sites have preserved wooden tools and structures, textiles, plant foods, and other organic materials that rarely survive in drier archaeological contexts, making these sites a uniquely valuable window into prehistoric material culture.

The Cortaillod culture was part of the broader Swiss lake-dwelling tradition (the Pfahlbauten) recognized as a UNESCO World Heritage Site, and it connects to the wider western European Neolithic cultural landscape. Its communities participated in exchange networks distributing flint, jade, copper, and other prestige materials across the Alpine region and beyond, contributing to the cultural interconnectedness of Neolithic Europe.
